original_abstract:
Rapid advancements in optical sensor technology have driven an increasing demand for processing high-resolution visual data. In particular, the prevalence of devices capable of capturing ultra-high definition (UHD) images and videos has created significant challenges for traditional image processing pipelines. In this paper, we address the challenge of low-light image enhancement (LLIE) by introducing a large-scale database featuring images at 4K and 8K resolutions. This dataset serves as the foundation for a systematic benchmarking study, where we provide a comprehensive evaluation of current LLIE algorithms.

Building on these insights, we propose LLFormer, a transformer-based framework designed specifically for low-light enhancement. At the heart of LLFormer are two novel components: axis-based multi-head self-attention and a cross-layer attention fusion block, both of which work to significantly reduce computational complexity to linear levels. Extensive experiments on our new UHD dataset and existing public benchmarks demonstrate that LLFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, we show that using LLIE models trained on our benchmark as a preprocessing step substantially boosts the performance of downstream tasks, such as face detection in poorly lit environments. Our source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/TaoWangzj/LLFormer.
humanized_abstract:
In recent years, optical sensors have progressed rapidly, with the need to process increasingly large amounts of visual data becoming more important. With the increasing prevalence of 4K and 8K UHD displays on various devices like cameras, TVs and even smartphones, processing UHD images and videos becomes a tremendous burden on current processing frameworks. In this work, a new large-scale low-light image enhancement (LLIE) dataset has been introduced, comprised of 4K and 8K UHD images to analyse and compare a series of state-of-the-art algorithms. A thorough benchmark is provided for each model which can serve as a reference for the industry.

Based on the above analysis, we introduce a new model called LLFormer, short for Low-Light Former, which is a transformer-based model that is optimized for low-light enhancement. The main contributions of this paper are: 1) two low computational cost operations are introduced, i.e., axis-based multi-head self-attention and cross-layer attention fusion block; 2) We design a new UHD low-light enhancement dataset, and use this dataset as well as the public available low-light enhancement benchmarks to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed model LLFormer, when comparing it with state-of-the-art methods. In addition, we show that models for low-light image and video enhancement trained on our proposed low-light enhancement benchmark can be used as efficient image preprocessing stage that improves performance of various downstream tasks such as face detection. Our source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/TaoWangzj/LLFormer.

document.output:
In recent years, optical sensors have progressed rapidly, with the need to process increasingly large amounts of visual data becoming more important. With the increasing prevalence of 4K and 8K UHD displays on various devices like cameras, TVs and even smartphones, processing UHD images and videos becomes a tremendous burden on current processing frameworks. In this work, a new large-scale low-light image enhancement (LLIE) dataset has been introduced, comprised of 4K and 8K UHD images to analyse and compare a series of state-of-the-art algorithms. A thorough benchmark is provided for each model which can serve as a reference for the industry.

Based on the above analysis, we introduce a new model called LLFormer, short for Low-Light Former, which is a transformer-based model that is optimized for low-light enhancement. The main contributions of this paper are: 1) two low computational cost operations are introduced, i.e., axis-based multi-head self-attention and cross-layer attention fusion block; 2) We design a new UHD low-light enhancement dataset, and use this dataset as well as the public available low-light enhancement benchmarks to demonstrate the superiority of our proposed model LLFormer, when comparing it with state-of-the-art methods. In addition, we show that models for low-light image and video enhancement trained on our proposed low-light enhancement benchmark can be used as efficient image preprocessing stage that improves performance of various downstream tasks such as face detection. Our source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/TaoWangzj/LLFormer.
document.input:
Rapid advancements in optical sensor technology have driven an increasing demand for processing high-resolution visual data. In particular, the prevalence of devices capable of capturing ultra-high definition (UHD) images and videos has created significant challenges for traditional image processing pipelines. In this paper, we address the challenge of low-light image enhancement (LLIE) by introducing a large-scale database featuring images at 4K and 8K resolutions. This dataset serves as the foundation for a systematic benchmarking study, where we provide a comprehensive evaluation of current LLIE algorithms.

Building on these insights, we propose LLFormer, a transformer-based framework designed specifically for low-light enhancement. At the heart of LLFormer are two novel components: axis-based multi-head self-attention and a cross-layer attention fusion block, both of which work to significantly reduce computational complexity to linear levels. Extensive experiments on our new UHD dataset and existing public benchmarks demonstrate that LLFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, we show that using LLIE models trained on our benchmark as a preprocessing step substantially boosts the performance of downstream tasks, such as face detection in poorly lit environments. Our source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/TaoWangzj/LLFormer.
